summary: DHH reflects on the importance of getting enough sleep rather than trying to squeeze more hours out of the day. He argues that skimping on sleep degrades the quality of all waking hours, making the trade-off counterproductive. Writing from personal experience of a short night's sleep, he describes feeling lethargic and unproductive. He concludes that protecting sleep quality is essential to making waking hours worthwhile.
key_points: Getting 8-9 hours of sleep is non-negotiable for productive, enjoyable waking hours
Two extra waking hours aren't worth it if they significantly lower the quality of the remaining hours
Sleep deprivation leads to lethargy and susceptibility to mindless distractions like bad television
Quality of hours matters more than quantity of hours
money_quotes: What good does two hours extra a day if it signaficantly lowers the quality of the rest of the hours?
Treasure the waken hours. Don't dilute their quality by adding more. It's going to cost you.
I've come to terms with that because I value the quality of hours while I'm awake.
key_insight: Maximizing productive output means protecting sleep rather than sacrificing it for more hours, because quality of time matters more than quantity.
